claim: “For #Covid vaccines, shingles and even more dangerous and painful skin conditions may be the new thrombocytopenia."

result.claim: “For #Covid vaccines, shingles and even more dangerous and painful skin conditions may be the new thrombocytopenia."
result.reasoning:
The claim suggests that shingles and other dangerous skin conditions may be a new side effect of COVID vaccines, comparable to thrombocytopenia.

Regarding shingles (herpes zoster), one case series (Paper ID: 58) reported 6 cases of herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases (AIIRD). The prevalence of HZ in this AIIRD patient group was 1.2% compared to 0% in controls. The paper notes that in most cases, the HZ infection was mild, and concludes that "Epidemiologic studies on the safety of the mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines in patients with AIIRD are needed to clarify the association between the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccination and reactivation of zoster." This indicates a possible association in a specific patient population, but the evidence is limited to a small case series and calls for further research to establish a clear link. It does not suggest a widespread or severe issue.

Regarding "even more dangerous and painful skin conditions," no specific evidence was found to support this part of the claim in relation to COVID vaccines.

Regarding the comparison to "thrombocytopenia," searches for a link between COVID vaccines and thrombocytopenia did not yield any relevant propositions or chunks of information. Therefore, there is no evidence in the knowledge base to support the idea that COVID vaccines cause thrombocytopenia, which undermines the comparison made in the claim.

In summary, while there is limited, suggestive evidence for herpes zoster reactivation in a specific patient group after COVID vaccination, the evidence does not support the claim of "even more dangerous and painful skin conditions" or the comparison to thrombocytopenia as a vaccine side effect. The available evidence is insufficient to confirm the claim as stated.

evidence.source: Unusual Response of Acute Monocytic Leukemia to Dandelion Root Extract
